item.title: New Zealand Yields Rise to Highest in a Year on Outlook Cut, Oil
item.description: New Zealand bond yields rose to the highest in about a year after the country’s AA+ credit rating outlook was cut to negative by Fitch Ratings.

item.title: Fonterra Raises Guidance as Conflict Poses Risk to Supply Chain
item.description: Fonterra Cooperative Group, the world’s biggest dairy exporter, lifted full-year guidance while warning that the Middle East conflict poised a risk to supply chains in the second half.

item.title: Traders Brace for Turbulent Open as War Rages On
item.description: Investors are bracing for another turbulent session as the US war in Iran enters a fourth week with no signs of easing.

item.title: America Movil’s Claro to Buy Desktop SA in $750 Million Deal
item.description: Claro Telecom Participacoes SA will buy a majority stake in telecom company Desktop SA for an enterprise value of 4 billion reais ($750 million), the companies announced Sunday.

item.title: Poste Italiane Launches €10.8 Billion Telecom Italia Bid
item.description: Poste Italiane SpA said it will launch a public offer valued at about €10.8 billion ($12.5 billion) for Telecom Italia SpA, seeking full control of the former phone monopoly as the state-backed group expands further into digital infrastructure, according to a statement.

item.title: Paschi Said to Review Lovaglio Position at Monday Board Meeting
item.description: Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena SpA’s board will discuss whether Luigi Lovaglio can remain chief executive officer after he agreed to stand for another term under a rival proposal by a minority shareholder, people familiar with the matter said.

item.title: Fannie, Freddie Place Large Bids for Mortgage-Backed Securities
item.description: Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac have begun placing sizable orders to purchase mortgage-backed securities, stepping into a market roiled by widening bond spreads amid a surge in volatility, according to a person with direct knowledge of the matter.

item.title: Bessent Says US to Press Ahead in Iran War With Hormuz in Sights
item.description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said US-Israeli attacks on Iran are aimed at destroying its fortifications along the Strait of Hormuz while Iranian leaders face a Monday deadline from President Donald Trump to reopen the waterway.

item.title: Options Market Reverts to 2022 Playbook for Iran War Risks
item.description: Investors are looking back at 2022 for clues on how the risk from the Iran war unfolds across equity markets.

item.title: Russia Launches Cargo Ship to ISS From Baikonur After Repairs
item.description: Russia launched an uncrewed cargo spacecraft toward the International Space Station on Sunday after recent repairs to the Baikonur Cosmodrome launch site, NASA said.

item.title: Hedge Fund Fermat Pushes Back Against EU Plan to Limit Retail Investors’ Access to Catastrophe Bonds
item.description: Fermat Capital Management says a European proposal to limit retail investors’ access to catastrophe bonds faces serious pushback, as money managers specialized in insurance-linked strategies mount a coordinated front.

item.title: Bitcoin Drops Below $69,000 as War Fears Weigh on Crypto Markets
item.description: Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies declined anew as the US, Israel and Iran traded fresh threats and attacks.

item.title: Big Tech’s Cause for Hope: Link Between Mag 7, S&P 500 Is Broken
item.description: For most of its three-year bull run, the S&P 500 Index has moved in lockstep with the shares of technology giants. But the relationship is suddenly breaking down — and that could be good news for languishing tech stocks.

item.title: Wall Street’s Favorite Emerging-Market Bet Falters as War Rages
item.description: Once a top pick among emerging-market investors, local-currency debt is quickly becoming a pain trade.

item.title: HORMUZ TRACKER: China-Bound LPG Carrier Joins Sparse Transits
item.description: Ship traffic through the Strait of Hormuz remains disrupted, with only a handful of Iran-linked vessels transiting the chokepoint as it enters a fourth week of effective closure.

item.title: Trump Planned for Big Oil’s Global Dominance. Then He Went to War With Iran
item.description: The conflict carries heightened risk for energy companies as executives gather for the CERAWeek conference in Houston.

item.title: Apple’s AirPods Max 2 Blurs the Lines Between Marketing and Innovation
item.description: Also: The company hikes prices on external storage.

item.title: UK Should Consider Temporary Cap on Energy Profits, Adviser Says
item.description: The UK should consider temporarily capping the profits of energy companies to protect consumers as prices spike in the wake of the Iran conflict, a top government adviser said on Sunday.

item.title: Sinopec’s Full-Year Profit Falls as Fuel Demand Weakens
item.description: Sinopec reported a steeper-than-expected decline in profit for 2025 as faltering fuel demand and an over-saturated chemicals market sapped margins.

item.title: Retailers Push UK to Join US and EU Ending Shein Parcel Loophole
item.description: British retailers are pushing the UK government to act faster and end a tax loophole that has helped online giants Shein and Temu make significant inroads and gain market share.

item.title: Hormuz Strait Closure Forcing Trump, Importers to Seek Solution
item.description: The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz — entering its fourth week as conflict in the region deepens — is piling pressure on the US administration and on major importers of oil, gas and fuels from the Persian Gulf, prompting them to scramble for solutions.

item.title: Iran Threatens to Hit Key Infrastructure After Trump’s Ultimatum
item.description: Iran on Sunday warned it will attack key infrastructure across the Middle East if President Donald Trump follows through on his threat to “obliterate“ Tehran’s power plants unless the Strait of Hormuz swiftly reopens.

item.title: South Korea Nominates BIS Official Shin as New BOK Governor
item.description: South Korea’s President Lee Jae Myung nominated Shin Hyun Song, head of the monetary and economic department at the Bank for International Settlements, as the new Bank of Korea governor, the presidential office said.

item.title: Unexpected Benefit of UK Banknote Innovation: Britons Eat Less Cash
item.description: A row over the decision to remove wartime leader Winston Churchill from the UK’s money last week exposed a country set in its ways — at least where its cherished pound notes are concerned.

item.title: Musk Says Tesla, SpaceX, xAI Chip Project to Kick Off in Texas
item.description: Elon Musk said his Terafab project — a grand plan to eventually manufacture his own chips for robotics, artificial intelligence and space data centers — will be built in Austin and jointly run by Tesla and SpaceX.

item.title: Apple CEO Praises China Partners as Beijing Applies Pressure
item.description: Apple Inc. Chief Executive Officer Tim Cook commended Chinese developers and the company’s partners in the country after a week of rising pressures in one of its biggest markets.

item.title: Japan Says Not Considering Unilateral Talks With Iran on Hormuz
item.description: Japan’s Foreign Minister Toshimitsu Motegi said the nation isn’t considering unilateral negotiations with Iran to secure passage for its vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, following a report that Tehran is prepared to grant the access.

item.title: Cuba Suffers Second Blackout in a Week Amid Fuel Squeeze
item.description: Cuba’s electricity grid suffered another blackout on Saturday, the second major hit to its energy system this week, amid the US administration fuel embargo and pressure on the Havana government.

item.title: Trump Gives Iran 48 Hours on Hormuz, Threatens Power Plants
item.description: President Donald Trump threatened to attack Iran’s power plants if the country didn’t swiftly reopen the Strait of Hormuz to commercial ship traffic after the passage of oil and gas cargoes has been paralyzed.

item.title: Shockwave of War Is Rippling Through the Global Economy
item.description: The global economy’s first collective health check since war broke out in the Middle East will arrive in the form of business surveys from the US to the euro zone.
